1.description: Private equity activity in insurance This figure shows the dollar value of PE activity in life insurance by year. Acquisitions are shown as positive numbers, in the year of the transaction. Subsequent exits are shown as negative numbers, also in the year of the initial transaction. We exclude exits after which PE firms retain significant control or continue to derive capital via investment management agreements. The regulatory treatment of private-label ABS changed as of year-end 2009, represented by a dashed vertical line.

2.description: Quarterly portfolio composition in event time This figure shows PE-backed insurers’ portfolio composition relative to non-PE-backed insurers at a quarterly frequency in event time. Both panels are based on regressions specified in Equation (2) using observations at the insurer-quarter level for 2007Q4–2014Q4 (capital treatment of private-label ABS changed starting 2009Q4), dropping insurers that are always PE-owned in this period and restricting to insurer-quarters with positive private-label ABS holdings (same sample as Table 4). Dependent variables are asset class shares (Private-label ABS/General account bonds for panel A and alternative NAIC categories 3–6/Private-label ABS for panel B). These shares are in percentage points and are winsorized at the 5th and 95th percentiles within type (PE, non-PE). Regressions include quarter and insurer fixed effects. Standard errors are double-clustered by insurer (NAIC code) and quarter. The figure shows point estimates as well as 95% confidence intervals.

3.description: Weekly portfolio composition in event time This figure shows PE-backed insurers’ portfolio composition relative to non-PE-backed insurers at a weekly frequency in event time. Both panels are based on the regressions specified in Equation (3) using observations at the insurer-week level for 2007Q4–2014Q4 (capital treatment of private-label ABS changed starting 2009Q4), dropping insurers that are always PE-owned in this period and restricting to insurer-quarters with positive private-label ABS holdings. Dependent variables are asset class shares (private-label ABS with alternate NAIC category higher than reported NAIC category (panel A) or all private-label ABS (panel B), both scaled by total general account bonds in the previous quarter). These shares are in percentage points and are winsorized at the 5th and 95th percentiles within type (PE, non-PE). Regressions include week and insurer fixed effects. Standard errors are double-clustered by insurer (NAIC code) and quarter. The figure shows point estimates as well as 95% confidence intervals.

4.description: Daily private-label ABS transactions in event time This figure shows daily transactions in private-label ABS for PE firms (panel A) and non-PE firms (panel B) in event time. Acquisition dates are represented by dashed vertical lines. The horizontal axis shows the number of days since and from acquisition. For non-PE firms we use transactions by all non-PE firms on the same dates. For each date in event time, we plot averages across PE firms and non-PE firms after winsorizing at the 10th and 90th percentiles. We impute an alternate NAIC category for private-label ABS based on ratings from Moody’s. Private-label ABS are separated into four categories. First, private-label ABS reported as NAIC 1, with an alternate NAIC category from 4 to 6. Second, all other private-label ABS, where the alternate NAIC category is higher than the reported NAIC category. Third, private-label ABS where we are unable to match ratings from Moody’s. Fourth, all other private-label ABS, where the alternate NAIC category is either the same as or lower than the reported NAIC category. For each category, cumulative net purchases are shown as a fraction of all general account bonds held as of the quarter-end prior to acquisition in percentage points.

5.description: New shadow reinsurance contracts This figure shows new shadow reinsurance contracts by year using data from A.M. Best. Shadow reinsurance contracts are with affiliated entities not rated by A.M. Best and not authorized by U.S. insurance regulators. We separate shadow insurance by whether it covers life insurance or annuities, and further break out annuity shadow reinsurance by whether it is reported as general account (fixed) or not (variable). Panel A shows all new shadow reinsurance by type. Panel B shows new fixed annuity reinsurance based on whether the insurer is owned by PE group A, owned by a different PE firm, or not PE owned.

6.description: Pricing of 5-year guaranteed annuities This figure shows quotes for multiyear guaranteed annuities (MYGAs) with guarantees for 5 years. It uses a single cross-section as of April 7, 2017, from Annuity Rate Watch. The sample is restricted to insurers with A.M. Best rating of A- or better (144 quotes). Observations are at the quote level (e.g., one PE group subsidiary quotes eight 5-year MYGAs with different conditions). The guaranteed yield is winsorized at the 5th and 95th percentiles. Panel A shows the distribution of all of these quotes, separately showing quotes by PE insurers. Panel B shows only high-band or mid-band MYGAs with market value adjustments (MVAs).

7.description: RBC ratios (2014) This figure shows RBC ratios in percentage points, capped above at 600 and below at 10, as of 2014. The RBC ratio is statutory capital (total adjusted capital) scaled by required capital (2 × ACL RBC). Insurers must submit corrective action plans if their RBC ratio is below 150% and has a negative trend, or breaches 100%. The 150% threshold is represented by a dashed line. Insurers with an RBC ratio below 100% can be required to take corrective action, while at an RBC ratio below 50% regulators are authorized to take control. The figure excludes insurers rated A or higher, as all PE insurers are rated A- or lower. Non-PE insurers are broken out by A.M. Best rating. Only PE insurers rated A- or B++ by A.M. Best are shown. For PE insurers, adjusted RBC ratios are also shown. Adjusted RBC ratios use a lower-bound estimate of the level of capital that would have been required under the old rating-based treatment of private-label ABS as the denominator.
